Now bowel injury, whether it is an open or laparoscopy, the principles of
bowel injury repair remains the same, that is freshen the edges and close transversely.
Now suture material of choice, previously KatGut was in vogue, but now we are using first layer with Vicryl and some people prefer using a non-absorbable like silk for second layer.
However, you can close both the layers
with the vicryl itself.
As you can see here, bleeding is actually a good sign.
You know, we should not coagulate because it says that there is a good amount of blood supply.
Here we have held one end of the bowel with an atraumatic grasper and with the other end I am controlling the other end of the bowel.
We have freshened out the edges and we closed first layer with
3-0 Vicryl in continuous manner.
Remember to close transversely.
Never ever we should close longitudinally which will definitely cause the stricture.
So you may have a intestinal obstruction later.
So you have to be gentle, you have to be accurate and you have to be watertight as far as closing the bowel is concerned.
So as you can see, I'm using 3-0 Vicryl.
We have to use, whether you can also give a suture with intermittent stitches, but here we chose to suture continuously.
Between two sutures, the distance should be less than a five millimeter.
You cannot have a leak from the anastomotic site.
We prefer to lock the suture just to make sure that the approximation is good.
Here I am locking.
And we continue to suture in a continuous manner.
If you have a very good suturing skills, most of these injuries can be managed laparoscopically.
If we do not possess the suturing skills, then just for putting three or four sutures, you will end up doing a laparotomy, which will delay the post-operative recovery of the patient.
This patient actually did very well.
stopped draining after two days.

We just added metanidazole with whatever ceftriaxone she was taking.
